light.explanation: Light roasts are denser and need higher temperature (92–95°C) to fully extract floral and fruity compounds.

medium.explanation: Medium roast is the sweet spot — no temperature adjustment needed.

medium-dark.explanation: Slightly lower temperature reduces extraction of bitter compounds developed in medium-dark roasts.

dark.explanation: Dark roasts extract quickly and bitterly at high temperatures. Lower temp preserves sweetness.

mild.explanation: Mild strength uses more water per gram of coffee — a higher ratio means a lighter cup.

balanced.explanation: Balanced strength follows the recipe's default ratio.

strong.explanation: Strong strength reduces the ratio — less water per gram of coffee for a bolder extraction.

espresso.explanation: Espresso-style uses a very low ratio (near 1:10) for intense, concentrated flavor.

extra-coarse.explanation: Extra-coarse grind has very low surface area — needs significantly more time for full extraction.

coarse.explanation: Coarse grind slows water flow through grounds — adds time to reach full extraction.

medium-coarse.explanation: Slightly coarser than medium — minor time increase for proper extraction.

medium.explanation: Medium grind is the baseline — no time adjustment needed.

medium-fine.explanation: Finer grind extracts faster — slight time reduction to prevent over-extraction.

fine.explanation: Fine grind has high surface area — reduces brew time to avoid bitterness.

extra-fine.explanation: Very fine grind (espresso-level) extracts extremely fast — significant time reduction.
